messages.content: A Paternoster lift is a type of elevator that consists of a chain of interconnected open compartments that move up and down through the lift shaft. Unlike traditional elevators, a Paternoster lift operates continuously and does not have buttons to select a floor. Instead, passengers must time their entry and exit to coincide with the compartment's movement, and can ride continuously up and down the building. Due to safety concerns, many Paternoster lifts have been decommissioned and replaced with traditional elevators.

Paternoster elevators are considered less safe compared to traditional elevators because they lack doors and require careful timing. Passengers must step in and out of the moving compartments when the elevator reaches their desired floor. Misjudging the timing or not following the proper procedure can lead to accidents, such as falling or getting caught between compartments. Additionally, the open design of the Paternoster lift can pose a safety hazard for children, people with mobility issues, or those carrying large objects. As a result, many buildings have replaced Paternoster lifts with more modern and safer elevator systems.
Why would pose a safety risk to children?

Paternoster lifts can pose a risk to children because they lack doors and have an open design. Children may be attracted to the moving compartments and attempt to climb in or out when they are in motion, which can lead to danger. Moreover, the constantly moving compartments of Paternoster elevators can be difficult for children to navigate, especially if they are not accompanied by an adult. Additionally, the height of the lift from the ground floor can be intimidating for children, and they may struggle to board or exit the lift without help. All of these factors make Paternoster lifts potentially dangerous for children, and it is one of the reasons why they are being phased out in many parts of the world.
What happens if you don't time it right?

If you don't time it right, riding a Paternoster lift can be risky. Since Paternoster lifts do not have doors and continuously move around the shaft, there is always a possibility of accidents occurring if you do not board or exit the compartment properly. Timing it wrong can lead to the following dangers:

1. Falling - if you attempt to step in or out of a moving compartment, you risk falling and injuring yourself.

2. Getting trapped - if you get caught in between the compartments or in the lift shaft, the result can be significant injury or possibly even death.

3. Panic and disorientation - because the Paternoster lift operates continuously, if you don't time it right, you may become disoriented and confused, which can lead to panic and stress.

In sum, you should always exercise caution when using a Paternoster lift and follow the proper procedures to avoid accidents.
Also, if you don't get out by the last floor, you can encounter a gear which can cause injury.

Yes, that's correct. Another danger of the Paternoster lift that can happen if you don't time it right is that once the passenger reaches the bottom or top of the lift and does not exit the compartment on time, they risk getting crushed or hit by the gears and moving parts of the lift. These parts are moving continuously to bring the next compartment in position to move in or out of the floor. Because the compartments of a Paternoster lift have no doors and are continually moving, they make it nearly impossible for people to know when to exit; thereby, creating the risk of encountering the gears. Thus, passengers must pay attention to the floor they are on and exit the lift promptly to avoid accidents.
Why would people not know when to exit?

People may not know when to exit from a Paternoster lift because it operates differently than a traditional elevator. In a traditional elevator, passengers use buttons to select the exact floor they want to go to, and the elevator stops there. In contrast, Paternoster lifts have a continuous loop of compartments that move up and down without stopping, and passengers must time their exit correctly. 

The absence of floor buttons in Paternoster lifts means that passengers have to rely on their observation of the floors passing by to determine when to get off. However, the compartments move at a fairly high speed, which may make it difficult for some people to correctly time their exit. Additionally, passengers who may be disoriented or unfamiliar with the lift's operation may not know when to exit.

All these factors can contribute to passengers not knowing when to exit the lift, increasing the risk of accidents and injury; therefore, it's essential to exercise caution and follow proper procedures when riding a Paternoster lift.
Well, these lifts usually have floor numbers on them.

Yes, that's correct. Many Paternoster lifts have floor indicators that help passengers know which floor they are on. Some of the lifts have an illuminated floor number displayed on a panel or screen visible from inside, while others display floor numbers on the outer facade of the compartments.

However, the rate at which Paternoster lifts move between floors can make it difficult for passengers to exit in time, even if they are aware of the floor numbers. Also, since the operation of the Paternoster lift is different from traditional lifts, using these lifts requires a certain level of experience and familiarity, which many people may not have.

Therefore, it is essential to be extra cautious when using a Paternoster lift and to follow all the proper procedures as prescribed.

The rate at which Paternoster lifts move between floors can vary depending on the specific model and design of the lift. Generally, Paternoster lifts move vertically at a constant rate and operate at a speed of about 0.3 to 0.5 meters per second, which is approximately 1 to 2 feet per second. This speed may not sound very fast, but it is enough to make it challenging for some passengers to exit the lift safely.

Additionally, since Paternoster lifts operate on a continuous loop of interconnected compartments, there may be little to no time for passengers to move in and out of individual compartments. This makes it important for passengers to be aware of their surroundings and to time their entry and exit to coincide with the movement of the compartments to avoid accidents.

In summary, passengers need to be very cautious while entering or exiting the Paternoster elevator because of its design and operations, no matter the speed, to prevent accidents and ensure their safety.
I think a better alternative would be to stop at each floor for a few seconds and include a door which automatically opens and closes when the elevator stops.

Yes, that's a great suggestion. Providing a set of doors and stopping the lift at each floor for a few seconds will make the Paternoster lift safer and more user-friendly by eliminating the risk of accidents associated with its open design.

Having a door that opens and closes automatically and holding the compartments long enough for passengers to get in and out will make the lift safer and easier to use. It will also provide passengers more time and generate confidence, enabling them to navigate in and out of the compartments more securely.

However, such modifications and upgrade to Paternoster elevators may be technically challenging and costly to the building owners. Due to the high cost of upgrades and modifications, many building owners have opted to dismantle the Paternoster elevators in favor of conventional ones. Nevertheless, the suggestions you made would certainly make the Paternoster lifts safer for users.
